symbols.name: n
symbols.def: Sample size.

symbols.name: O
symbols.space: {(Y,D,X): Y in R, D in {0,1}, X in X}
symbols.def: Observed unit O = (Y, D, X).

symbols.name: O_1, ..., O_n
symbols.space: ({(Y,D,X): Y in R, D in {0,1}, X in X})^n
symbols.def: Observed sample of n units.

symbols.name: Y
symbols.def: Observed outcome component of O.

symbols.name: D
symbols.def: Observed treatment indicator component of O.

symbols.name: X
symbols.def: Observed covariate component of O.

symbols.name: P
symbols.def: Observed-data law of O at sample size n.

symbols.name: P_X
symbols.def: Marginal law of X induced by P.

symbols.name: Y(1), Y(0)
symbols.def: Potential outcomes under treatment and control.

symbols.name: kappa
symbols.def: One-sided overlap-decay exponent governing shell probabilities near e(X) = 0.

symbols.name: eta_plus
symbols.def: Right-overlap margin satisfying e(X) <= 1 - eta_plus almost surely.

symbols.name: eta_minus
symbols.def: Left-overlap margin for the strict-overlap sanity reduction.

symbols.name: lambda_0
symbols.def: Fixed upper cap for the clipping grid, chosen inside the tail region where the shell law is imposed.

symbols.name: r_1n
symbols.def: Oracle L2(P_X) estimability rate for the treated regression nuisance g_1.

symbols.name: r_0n
symbols.def: Oracle L2(P_X) estimability rate for the control regression nuisance g_0.

symbols.name: r_en
symbols.def: Oracle L2(P_X) estimability rate for the propensity nuisance e.

symbols.name: hat_g_1n
symbols.def: Cross-fitted witness estimator chosen for the upper theorem to attain the treated-regression estimability rate.

symbols.name: hat_g_0n
symbols.def: Cross-fitted witness estimator chosen for the upper theorem to attain the control-regression estimability rate.

symbols.name: hat_e_n
symbols.def: Cross-fitted witness estimator chosen for the upper theorem to attain the propensity estimability rate before clipping and right-side stabilization.

symbols.name: lambda
symbols.def: Lower clipping threshold for the treated inverse-propensity factor.

symbols.name: lambda_dagger_n
symbols.def: Deterministic near-minimizing clipping threshold used by the converse, chosen so that R_n(lambda_dagger_n) <= 2 R_n^*.

symbols.name: tbar_n
symbols.def: Largest polynomial clipping exponent with tbar_n -> infinity.

symbols.name: a_0
symbols.def: Exponent satisfying r_0n = n^(-a_0) in the rate-specialized subclass.

symbols.name: a_1
symbols.def: Exponent satisfying r_1n = n^(-a_1) in the rate-specialized subclass.

symbols.name: a_e
symbols.def: Exponent satisfying r_en = n^(-a_e) in the rate-specialized subclass.

assumptions.condition: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
Y = D Y(1) + (1 - D) Y(0) almost surely.
(Y(1), Y(0)) is independent of D conditional on X.

There exist c_shell_minus > 0, c_shell_plus > 0, and lambda_0 in (0,1) such that c_shell_minus lambda^kappa <= P(lambda / 2 < e(X) <= lambda) <= c_shell_plus lambda^kappa for every lambda in (0, lambda_0].

novel.justification: This is realistic when the propensity score has a density f_e(u) comparable to u^(kappa - 1) near zero, because then each dyadic shell (lambda / 2, lambda] has mass of order lambda^kappa. That class includes Beta-type boundary behavior and monotone single-index selection rules with regularly varying latent-index density at the participation cutoff.

This is realistic in one-sided weak-overlap designs where treatment scarcity occurs only near e(X) = 0 while the control arm stays uniformly represented. Examples include eligibility rules with a rare-treatment tail but a control take-up rate bounded away from zero, and Beta-type propensity laws that are singular only at the left boundary.

This is realistic in one-sided weak-overlap designs that separate the rare-treatment boundary region from a regular interior region. Any design with left-tail irregularity confined below lambda_0 and a control-rich interior beginning strictly above 2 lambda_0 satisfies it, including Beta-type tails spliced to an interior propensity band.

assumptions.condition: sup_{x in X} E[(Y - g_1(X))^2 | D = 1, X = x] <= sigma_1_sq for some finite sigma_1_sq.
sup_{x in X} E[(Y - g_0(X))^2 | D = 0, X = x] <= sigma_0_sq for some finite sigma_0_sq.
The nuisance g_1 is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_1n from an i.i.d. sample of O.

standard.name: structure-agnostic treated-regression estimability at rate r_1n

assumptions.condition: The nuisance g_0 is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_0n from an i.i.d. sample of O.

standard.name: structure-agnostic control-regression estimability at rate r_0n

assumptions.condition: The nuisance e is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_en from an i.i.d. sample of O.

standard.name: structure-agnostic propensity estimability at rate r_en

assumptions.condition: There exists c_reg > 0 such that P(2 lambda_0 < e(X) <= 1 - eta_plus) >= c_reg.

novel.justification: This is realistic when the design is one-sidedly weak only near e(X) = 0 and retains a nonvanishing interior stratum with treatment propensity bounded away from both 0 and 1. Mixture designs with a rare-treatment tail plus a regular eligibility stratum satisfy this directly once the tail cap lambda_0 is chosen below the interior onset.

definitions.name: W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)
definitions.construction: { P : P admits potential outcomes (Y(1), Y(0)) and satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:tail-shell, ass:right-overlap, ass:region-compatibility, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e at sample size n }

definitions.name: W_n^reg(r_0n, r_1n, r_en)
definitions.construction: { P : P admits potential outcomes (Y(1), Y(0)) and satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:strict-left-overlap-sanity, ass:right-overlap, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e at sample size n }

definitions.name: W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)
definitions.construction: { P : P satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:tail-shell, ass:right-overlap, ass:region-compatibility, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, ass:estimable-e, and ass:regular-cell-mass at sample size n }

definitions.name: S_lambda
definitions.construction: S_lambda = { x in X : lambda / 2 < e(x) <= lambda }

definitions.name: B_lambda
definitions.construction: B_lambda = { x in X : 2 lambda < e(x) <= 1 - eta_plus }

definitions.name: e_lambda
definitions.construction: e_lambda(x) = max{e(x), lambda}

definitions.name: hat_e_lambda
definitions.construction: hat_e_lambda(x) = min{1 - eta_plus / 2, max{hat_e_n(x), lambda}}

definitions.name: psi_lambda
definitions.construction: psi_lambda(O; g_0, g_1, e) = g_1(X) - g_0(X) + D (Y - g_1(X)) / e_lambda(X) - (1 - D) (Y - g_0(X)) / (1 - e(X))

definitions.name: hat_psi_lambda
definitions.construction: hat_psi_lambda(O) = hat_g_1n(X) - hat_g_0n(X) + D (Y - hat_g_1n(X)) / hat_e_lambda(X) - (1 - D) (Y - hat_g_0n(X)) / (1 - hat_e_lambda(X))

definitions.name: hat_tau_lambda
definitions.construction: hat_tau_lambda = n^(-1) sum_{i=1}^n hat_psi_lambda(O_i; hat_g_0n^(-i), hat_g_1n^(-i), hat_e_n^(-i), eta_plus, lambda)

definitions.name: pi(lambda)
definitions.construction: pi(lambda) = P(e(X) <= lambda)

definitions.name: v_kappa(lambda)
definitions.construction: v_kappa(lambda) = lambda^(-(1 - kappa) / 2) if 0 < kappa < 1, v_kappa(lambda) = (1 + log(lambda_0 / lambda))^(1/2) if kappa = 1, and v_kappa(lambda) = 1 if kappa > 1

definitions.name: U_n(lambda)
definitions.construction: U_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) (E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)])^(1/2) + pi(lambda)^(1/2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en

definitions.name: R_n(lambda)
definitions.construction: R_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) v_kappa(lambda) + lambda^(kappa / 2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en

definitions.name: R_n^*
definitions.construction: R_n^* = inf_{lambda in Lambda_n} R_n(lambda)

definitions.name: lambda_dagger_n
definitions.construction: lambda_dagger_n in { lambda in Lambda_n : R_n(lambda) <= 2 R_n^* }

definitions.name: Lambda_n
definitions.construction: Lambda_n = { lambda_0 n^(-t) : 0 <= t <= tbar_n }

definitions.name: delta_1n(lambda)
definitions.construction: delta_1n(lambda) = r_1n / (16 P_X(S_lambda)^(1/2))

definitions.name: delta_0n(lambda)
definitions.construction: delta_0n(lambda) = r_0n / (16 P_X(B_lambda)^(1/2))

definitions.name: u_1n(lambda)
definitions.construction: u_1n(lambda) = r_en / (16 || e(X) (1 - e(X)) 1_{S_lambda}(X) ||_{L2(P_X)})

definitions.name: u_0n(lambda)
definitions.construction: u_0n(lambda) = r_en / (16 || e(X) (1 - e(X)) 1_{B_lambda}(X) ||_{L2(P_X)})

definitions.name: H_{n,lambda}
definitions.construction: Handle for a lower-bound family: start from a base law P in W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en); for each (s,t) in {-1,1}^2 define g_{1,s,t}(x) = g_1(x) + s delta_1n(lambda) 1_{S_lambda}(x), g_{0,s,t}(x) = g_0(x) + t delta_0n(lambda) 1_{B_lambda}(x), h_{s,t,lambda}(x) = s min{u_1n(lambda), log((1 - eta_plus / 2) / (eta_plus / 2)) - log(e(x) / (1 - e(x)))} 1_{S_lambda}(x) + t min{u_0n(lambda), log((1 - eta_plus / 2) / (eta_plus / 2)) - log(e(x) / (1 - e(x)))} 1_{B_lambda}(x), and e_{s,t}(x) = expit(log(e(x) / (1 - e(x))) + h_{s,t,lambda}(x)). This clipping map keeps e_{s,t}(x) <= 1 - eta_plus / 2 pointwise; the remaining open task is to verify bounded pairwise chi-square distance while keeping P_X and the centered residual laws fixed.

definitions.name: E_n(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa)
definitions.construction: { P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) : r_0n = n^(-a_0), r_1n = n^(-a_1), and r_en = n^(-a_e) }

definitions.name: E_n^reg(a_0, a_1, a_e)
definitions.construction: { P in W_n^reg(r_0n, r_1n, r_en) : r_0n = n^(-a_0), r_1n = n^(-a_1), and r_en = n^(-a_e) }

definitions.name: nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa)
definitions.construction: nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = sup_{t >= 0} min{ 1/2 - t (1 - kappa) / 2, a_1 + t kappa / 2, a_1 + a_e - t, a_0 + a_e } if 0 < kappa < 1, and nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = sup_{t >= 0} min{ 1/2, a_1 + t kappa / 2, a_1 + a_e - t, a_0 + a_e } if kappa >= 1

definitions.name: kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e)
definitions.construction: kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e) = inf{ kappa > 0 : nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e} }

statements.statement: Choose cross-fitted witness estimators hat_g_1n, hat_g_0n, and hat_e_n attaining the rates in ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e. Then for every law P satisfying ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:right-overlap, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e, and in particular for every P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) or P in W_n^reg(r_0n, r_1n, r_en), and every deterministic lambda in Lambda_n, E_P[psi_lambda(O; g_0, g_1, e)] = tau and |hat_tau_lambda - tau| = O_P(U_n(lambda)).
statements.justification: This theorem isolates the estimator-side object that must be matched by any minimax frontier claim: an exact clipped-score absolute-risk envelope before any tail-order simplification.
statements.gap: Relative to Dorn2025WeakOverlapDRTStats and MaSantAnnaSasakiUra2023DRWeakOverlap, this is an estimator-side refinement rather than a new causal target: Dorn's abstract studies weak-overlap Wald inference for a thresholded doubly robust estimator, and Ma-Sant'Anna-Sasaki-Ura study bias-corrected trimming for the original ATE. This statement instead extracts the structure-agnostic absolute-risk envelope U_n(lambda) that a minimax theorem would optimize.
statements.consumer: This is the upper benchmark needed to compare one-sided weak-overlap difficulty directly to the regular-overlap Jin-Syrgkanis and Bonvini-Kennedy-Dukes-Balakrishnan frontiers.

statements.statement: For every P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) and every lambda in Lambda_n there exist constants 0 < c_1 <= c_2 < infinity, depending only on the class constants, such that c_1 lambda^kappa <= pi(lambda) <= c_2 lambda^kappa, c_1 v_kappa(lambda)^2 <= E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)] <= c_2 v_kappa(lambda)^2, and c_1 R_n(lambda) <= U_n(lambda) <= c_2 R_n(lambda).
statements.justification: This is the algebraic bridge from primitive clipped-score quantities to the clean kappa-indexed rate functional that the phase diagram uses.
statements.gap: KhanTamer2010IrregularIdentification and the weak-overlap bookkeeping summarized in Dorn2025WeakOverlapDRTStats explain why inverse weights alter rate calculations, but they do not give this exact-to-order reduction for a clipped orthogonal ATE score with separate treated and control nuisance-product channels and the kappa = 1 normalization built into v_kappa(lambda).
statements.consumer: It makes the conjectured frontier directly comparable to published bounded-overlap rates by collapsing the primitive envelope to a single tail-order functional.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix \(P \in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})\) and \(\lambda \in \Lambda_n\). By Definition \(\Lambda_n=\{\lambda_0 n^{-t}:0\le t\le \bar t_n\}\), we have \(0<\lambda\le \lambda_0\). Write
\[
A_j(\lambda)=\{2^{-(j+1)}\lambda < e(X) \le 2^{-j}\lambda\},\qquad j=0,1,2,\dots .
\]
These sets are pairwise disjoint and their union is \(\{e(X)\le \lambda\}\). Applying Assumption `ass:tail-shell` with upper endpoint \(2^{-j}\lambda\) gives
\[
c_{\rm shell,-}\,2^{-j\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}
\le P_X\bigl(A_j(\lambda)\bigr)
\le c_{\rm shell,+}\,2^{-j\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}
\qquad (j\ge 0).
\]
Therefore
\[
\pi(\lambda)=P\{e(X)\le \lambda\}=\sum_{j\ge 0}P_X\bigl(A_j(\lambda)\bigr).
\]
Keeping only \(j=0\) yields the lower bound
\[
\pi(\lambda)\ge P_X\bigl(A_0(\lambda)\bigr)\ge c_{\rm shell,-}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]
Summing the upper bounds gives
\[
\pi(\lambda)
\le c_{\rm shell,+}\lambda^{\kappa}\sum_{j\ge 0}2^{-j\kappa}
= \frac{c_{\rm shell,+}}{1-2^{-\kappa}}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]
Hence there exist positive constants \(c_{\pi,1},c_{\pi,2}\), depending only on \((c_{\rm shell,-},c_{\rm shell,+},\kappa)\), such that
\[
c_{\pi,1}\lambda^{\kappa}\le \pi(\lambda)\le c_{\pi,2}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]

We next compare \(E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\) with \(v_\kappa(\lambda)^2\). Let \(J=J(\lambda)\) be the unique integer such that
\[
2^J\lambda\le \lambda_0<2^{J+1}\lambda.
\]
Then \(2^J\lambda>\lambda_0/2\). Decompose
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]
= \lambda^{-1}\pi(\lambda)
+ \sum_{j=0}^{J-1} E\!
\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}\right]
+ E\!
\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{e(X)>2^J\lambda\}\right].
\]
For \(0\le j\le J-1\), on the shell \(\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}\) we have
\[
(2^{j+1}\lambda)^{-1}
\le e(X)^{-1}
\le (2^j\lambda)^{-1}.
\]
Using `ass:tail-shell` with upper endpoint \(2^{j+1}\lambda\),
\[
c_{\rm shell,-}2^{(j+1)\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}
\le P\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}
\le c_{\rm shell,+}2^{(j+1)\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]
Hence there are constants \(0<\underline C\le \overline C<\infty\), depending only on the shell constants and \(\kappa\), such that for every \(0\le j\le J-1\),
\[
\underline C\,\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}
\le E\!
\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}\right]
\le \overline C\,\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}.
\]
The remainder term is always bounded above by a constant because \(e(X)>2^J\lambda>\lambda_0/2\) on that event, so
\[
0\le E\!\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{e(X)>2^J\lambda\}\right]\le 2/\lambda_0.
\]
We now treat the three regimes in Definition `def:variance-scale`.

If \(0<\kappa<1\), then \(\kappa-1<0\). The clipped term already gives the correct lower order:
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\ge \lambda^{-1}\pi(\lambda)\ge c_{\pi,1}\lambda^{\kappa-1}.
\]
For the upper bound,
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]
\le c_{\pi,2}\lambda^{\kappa-1}
+ \overline C\lambda^{\kappa-1}\sum_{j=0}^{J-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}
+ 2/\lambda_0.
\]
Because \(\sum_{j\ge 0}2^{j(\kappa-1)}<\infty\) and \(\lambda\le \lambda_0\) implies \(2/\lambda_0\le C\lambda^{\kappa-1}\), we obtain
\[
c_1'\lambda^{\kappa-1}\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_2'\lambda^{\kappa-1}.
\]
Since \(v_\kappa(\lambda)^2=\lambda^{-(1-\kappa)}=\lambda^{\kappa-1}\) in this regime, this is exactly
\[
c_1' v_\kappa(\lambda)^2\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_2' v_\kappa(\lambda)^2.
\]

If \(\kappa=1\), each shell contributes a positive constant order. Indeed the display above becomes
\[
\underline C\le E\!
\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}\right]
\le \overline C,
\qquad 0\le j\le J-1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\underline C J \le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}] \le c_{\pi,2}+\overline C J + 2/\lambda_0.
\]
Since \(2^J\lambda\le \lambda_0<2^{J+1}\lambda\), we have
\[
J\le \log_2(\lambda_0/\lambda)<J+1,
\]
so \(J+1\) is comparable, up to universal base-change constants, to \(1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)\). Thus
\[
c_1'\bigl(1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)\bigr)
\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]
\le c_2'\bigl(1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)\bigr).
\]
Because \(v_1(\lambda)^2=1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)\), the same display is
\[
c_1' v_1(\lambda)^2\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_2' v_1(\lambda)^2.
\]

If \(\kappa>1\), then the event \(\{\lambda_0/2<e(X)\le \lambda_0\}\) is contained in \(\{e(X)>\lambda\}\), because \(\lambda\le \lambda_0\). Hence
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]
\ge \lambda_0^{-1}P\{\lambda_0/2<e(X)\le \lambda_0\}
\ge c_{\rm shell,-}\lambda_0^{\kappa-1}.
\]
For the upper bound, the shell display gives
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{J-1}\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}
\le C\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{J(\kappa-1)}
\le C\lambda_0^{\kappa-1},
\]
because \(2^J\lambda\le \lambda_0\). Together with the clipped term and the remainder bound, this yields
\[
c_1'\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_2'.
\]
Since \(v_\kappa(\lambda)^2=1\) for \(\kappa>1\), we again have
\[
c_1' v_\kappa(\lambda)^2\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_2' v_\kappa(\lambda)^2.
\]

The last claim follows immediately. By Definition `def:exact-frontier-functional`,
\[
U_n(\lambda)=n^{-1/2}\bigl(E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\bigr)^{1/2}+\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}r_{1n}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
By Definition `def:frontier-functional`,
\[
R_n(\lambda)=n^{-1/2}v_\kappa(\lambda)+\lambda^{\kappa/2}r_{1n}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
We have already shown that \(\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}\) is comparable to \(\lambda^{\kappa/2}\) and that \((E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}])^{1/2}\) is comparable to \(v_\kappa(\lambda)\), with constants depending only on the class constants. The last two summands are identical in \(U_n(\lambda)\) and \(R_n(\lambda)\). Since all four summands are nonnegative, taking the minimum of the lower comparison constants and the maximum of the upper comparison constants gives positive finite \(c_1\le c_2\) such that
\[
c_1 R_n(\lambda)\le U_n(\lambda)\le c_2 R_n(\lambda).
\]
This proves all three claimed comparisons.

statements.statement: For every P in W_n^reg(r_0n, r_1n, r_en), if lambda in Lambda_n satisfies lambda < eta_minus, then e_lambda(x) = e(x) for all x in X and pi(lambda) = 0, so U_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) (E[e(X)^(-1)])^(1/2) + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en. In particular, if lambda_0 < eta_minus then choosing the fixed grid point lambda = lambda_0 yields |hat_tau_lambda - tau| = O_P(n^(-1/2) + r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en), which is the bounded-overlap structure-agnostic benchmark up to constants.
statements.justification: The proposal needs a concrete reduction showing that the weak-overlap machinery collapses to the regular-overlap benchmark when the left tail is ruled out.
statements.gap: JinSyrgkanis2024StructureAgnosticDR sets up ATE and ATT in Introduction equation (1), identifies ATE and weighted ATE under bounded overlap in equations (5)-(7), and states the bounded-overlap structure-agnostic rate benchmark in equation (8). This proposition is the exact reduction from the present one-sided shell model back to that same bounded-overlap benchmark.
statements.consumer: It certifies that any deterioration in the phase diagram is genuinely driven by one-sided overlap decay rather than by a relabeling of the regular-overlap problem.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix \(P\in W_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})\) and \(\lambda\in\Lambda_n\) with \(\lambda<\eta_-\). By Assumption `ass:strict-left-overlap-sanity`, \(e(X)\ge \eta_-\) almost surely. Hence \(e(x)\ge \eta_->\lambda\) for every \(x\), so Definition `def:true-clipped-propensity` gives
\[
e_\lambda(x)=\max\{e(x),\lambda\}=e(x)
\qquad\text{for all }x\in\mathcal X.
\]
Likewise, by Definition `def:tail-mass-functional`,
\[
\pi(\lambda)=P\{e(X)\le \lambda\}=0,
\]
because the event \(\{e(X)\le \lambda\}\) is empty almost surely under \(e(X)\ge \eta_->\lambda\).

Substituting these two identities into Definition `def:exact-frontier-functional`,
\[
U_n(\lambda)
= n^{-1/2}\bigl(E[e(X)^{-1}]\bigr)^{1/2}
+ \lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}
+ r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
This proves the first displayed formula.

For the benchmark reduction, assume now that \(\lambda_0<\eta_-\). Then the grid point \(\lambda=\lambda_0\) belongs to \(\Lambda_n\) by Definition `def:clipping-grid` (take \(t=0\)). Since \(P\in W_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})\), Theorem `thm:clipped-upper` applies and yields
\[
|\hat\tau_{\lambda_0}-\tau| = O_P\bigl(U_n(\lambda_0)\bigr).
\]
By the formula just proved,
\[
U_n(\lambda_0)
= n^{-1/2}\bigl(E[e(X)^{-1}]\bigr)^{1/2}
+ \lambda_0^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}
+ r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
Because \(e(X)\ge \eta_-\) almost surely, \(e(X)^{-1}\le \eta_-^{-1}\) almost surely, so
\[
E[e(X)^{-1}]\le \eta_-^{-1}<\infty.
\]
Also \(\lambda_0\) is a fixed positive constant. Therefore
\[
U_n(\lambda_0)=O\bigl(n^{-1/2}+r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}\bigr),
\]
and hence
\[
|\hat\tau_{\lambda_0}-\tau|
= O_P\bigl(n^{-1/2}+r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}\bigr).
\]
This is exactly the bounded-overlap structure-agnostic benchmark up to constants: the weak-overlap bias term \(\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}r_{1n}\) vanishes because the left tail is absent, and the variance term remains regular root-\(n\).

statements.statement: Let lambda_dagger_n be any deterministic selection satisfying def:near-optimal-threshold. Then the lower-bound handle H_{n,lambda_dagger_n} can be completed on the subclass W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) into a four-point Le Cam / Assouad family with pairwise chi-square distances uniformly bounded by a universal constant and with inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| >= c R_n^* for some universal c > 0.
statements.justification: The open converse is aligned to the frontier object itself: the handle is asked to realize a bounded-divergence family at a deterministic near-minimizing shell, so the lower bound targets R_n^* rather than a looser sup_lambda scale.
statements.gap: KennedyBalakrishnanRobinsWasserman2024MinimaxCATE states in the abstract that its minimax lower bound for pointwise CATE is obtained by a localized fuzzy-hypotheses construction, and JinSyrgkanis2025GeneralLinearFunctionalLowerBounds states in the abstract that it extends ATE lower bounds to a general class of functionals and two debiasing regimes. Neither paper localizes the experiment at a one-sided propensity shell or builds the treated/control asymmetric global-ATE family encoded by H_{n,lambda_dagger_n} and R_n^*.
statements.consumer: A completed converse would turn the upper envelope into a genuine equivalence frontier and would identify whether weak overlap changes the ATE difficulty class through variance, treated-regression, or nuisance-product channels.

statements.statement: The minimax absolute risk for tau over W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) is equivalent, up to universal constants, to R_n^* = inf_{lambda in Lambda_n} R_n(lambda).
statements.justification: This is the flagship equivalence frontier: same full-population ATE and same structure-agnostic nuisance-rate inputs as the regular-overlap literature, but with the overlap axis made explicit through kappa.
statements.gap: JinSyrgkanis2024StructureAgnosticDR introduces the bounded-overlap ATE, ATT, and weighted-ATE targets in Introduction equations (1) and (5)-(7) under m_0(X) in [c, 1 - c] almost surely and states the corresponding structure-agnostic benchmark in equation (8). BonviniKennedyDukesBalakrishnan2024StructureAgnosticATE states in the abstract that it derives ATE minimax lower bounds in pure structure-agnostic and hybrid smoothness classes. JinSyrgkanis2025GeneralLinearFunctionalLowerBounds states in the abstract that it generalizes the ATE lower bound to general functionals and proves DML optimality in two regimes. None of those paper-level statements is a one-sided shell-calibrated ATE frontier with overlap decay indexed by kappa and rate functional R_n^*.
statements.consumer: It would decide exactly when one-sided overlap decay preserves the published bounded-overlap frontier and when it forces a slower full-ATE rate.

statements.statement: Over E_n(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa), the conjectured minimax absolute rate is n^(-nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa)) when kappa != 1. When kappa = 1, the conjectured rate is n^(-nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, 1)) if nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, 1) < 1/2, and n^(-1/2) (log n)^(1/2) if nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, 1) = 1/2. One-sided overlap decay strictly changes the bounded-overlap structure-agnostic benchmark min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e} exactly when kappa < kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e), while kappa = 1 is a logarithmic boundary only in the root-n regime nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, 1) = 1/2.
statements.justification: The rate exponent map is the observable deliverable of the frontier claim: it shows where weak overlap is first-order and where it is only logarithmic or absent.
statements.gap: HongLeungLi2020FinitePopulationLimitedOverlap and HeilerKazak2021ExtremePropensity show that limited overlap changes rates or limit laws, but not this strict-deterioration map against the Jin-Syrgkanis / Bonvini-Kennedy-Dukes-Balakrishnan bounded-overlap benchmark with the kappa = 1 logarithm attached only when the variance channel is rate-determining.
statements.consumer: It gives a concrete phase diagram for deciding whether clipping or richer estimators are first-order necessary at a given nuisance smoothness profile.

statements.statement: Assume conj:minimax-frontier. On E_n^reg(a_0, a_1, a_e), if lambda_0 < eta_minus then prop:strict-overlap-sanity yields an estimator with absolute risk O_P(n^(-min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e})). If instead 0 < kappa < kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e), then n^(min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e}) inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in E_n(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| -> infinity.
statements.justification: This is the formal strict-extension certificate requested by the reviewer: it separates the bounded-overlap comparator class from the weak-tail class, then pairs the benchmark-rate upper bound on the former with strict deterioration on a named weak-overlap subclass.
statements.gap: JinSyrgkanis2024StructureAgnosticDR and BonviniKennedyDukesBalakrishnan2024StructureAgnosticATE give bounded-overlap ATE benchmarks, but neither paper states a theorem that juxtaposes that benchmark with a one-sided overlap-decay subclass under the same full-population ATE target and black-box nuisance-rate inputs. This proposition is the explicit comparator-tightening statement.
statements.consumer: It supplies the referee-facing theorem that the present kernel is not a relabeling of published bounded-overlap ATE theory.
statements.proof_tex:
Let
\[
B=\min\{1/2,\ a_1+a_e,\ a_0+a_e\}.
\]
We prove the two assertions in order.

First consider the regular-overlap class \(E_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(a_0,a_1,a_e)\). By Definition `def:regular-rate-exponent-subclass`, on this class the ambient nuisance-rate sequences satisfy
\[
r_{0n}=n^{-a_0},\qquad r_{1n}=n^{-a_1},\qquad r_{en}=n^{-a_e}.
\]
If \(\lambda_0<\eta_-\), Proposition `prop:strict-overlap-sanity` applies at the fixed grid point \(\lambda=\lambda_0\) and yields
\[
|\hat\tau_{\lambda_0}-\tau|
= O_P\bigl(n^{-1/2}+r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}\bigr)
= O_P\bigl(n^{-1/2}+n^{-(a_1+a_e)}+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}\bigr).
\]
Since the sum of finitely many power terms is of the same order as the largest one,
\[
n^{-1/2}+n^{-(a_1+a_e)}+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}=O\bigl(n^{-B}\bigr).
\]
Therefore the regular-overlap side of the proposition follows:
\[
|\hat\tau_{\lambda_0}-\tau|=O_P\bigl(n^{-B}\bigr).
\]
This is exactly the benchmark claimed in the statement.

Now consider the weak-overlap subclass \(E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\) and assume \(0<\kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)\). By the auxiliary lemma `lem:kappastar-gap`,
\[
\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)<B.
\]
In particular, if \(\kappa=1\) then automatically \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2\), because \(B\le 1/2\). Hence the auxiliary lemma `lem:frontier-power-rate` applies in all cases covered by \(0<\kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)\): either \(\kappa\neq 1\), or \(\kappa=1\) with \(\nu<1/2\). It gives constants \(0<c_R\le C_R<\infty\) such that
\[
c_R n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}
\le R_n^*
\le C_R n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]

Next we invoke `conj:minimax-frontier`. After specializing the ambient nuisance-rate sequences to
\[
r_{0n}=n^{-a_0},\qquad r_{1n}=n^{-a_1},\qquad r_{en}=n^{-a_e},
\]
Definition `def:rate-exponent-subclass` is exactly the weak-overlap class with those rate inputs, so the conjectured minimax comparison applies on \(E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\). Therefore there exists \(c_M>0\) such that
\[
\inf_{\hat\tau}\sup_{P\in E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|
\ge c_M R_n^*.
\]
Combining this with the lower bound on \(R_n^*\),
\[
\inf_{\hat\tau}\sup_{P\in E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|
\ge c_M c_R n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Multiply both sides by \(n^B\). Since \(B-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)>0\), we obtain
\[
n^B\inf_{\hat\tau}\sup_{P\in E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|
\ge c_M c_R n^{B-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}\to\infty.
\]
This is exactly the asserted strict deterioration relative to the bounded-overlap benchmark.

statements.statement: For every deterministic lambda in Lambda_n and every law P satisfying ass:consistency and ass:ignorability, E_P[psi_lambda(O; g_0, g_1, e)] = tau.
statements.proof_tex:
Fix any deterministic $\lambda \in \Lambda_n$. Under consistency and conditional ignorability, for each $d \in \{0,1\}$ we have
+\[
+g_d(X)=\mathbb E[Y\mid D=d,X]=\mathbb E[Y(d)\mid X]
+\]
+almost surely. Therefore
+\[
+\tau=\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)]=\mathbb E\big[\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X]\big]=\mathbb E[g_1(X)-g_0(X)].
+\]
+Now condition on $X$. Since $e_\lambda(X)$ is a function of $X$ alone,
+\[
+\mathbb E\!\left[\frac{D\{Y-g_1(X)\}}{e_\lambda(X)}\Bigm|X\right]
+=\frac{1}{e_\lambda(X)}\mathbb E\!\left[D\,\mathbb E\big[Y-g_1(X)\mid D,X\big]\Bigm|X\right]=0,
+\]
+because $\mathbb E[Y-g_1(X)\mid D=1,X]=0$ by the definition of $g_1$. Likewise,
+\[
+\mathbb E\!\left[\frac{(1-D)\{Y-g_0(X)\}}{1-e(X)}\Bigm|X\right]=0
+\]
+because $\mathbb E[Y-g_0(X)\mid D=0,X]=0$. Hence
+\[
+\mathbb E[\psi_\lambda(O;g_0,g_1,e)\mid X]=g_1(X)-g_0(X),
+\]
+and taking expectations yields
+\[
+\mathbb E_P[\psi_\lambda(O;g_0,g_1,e)]=\mathbb E[g_1(X)-g_0(X)]=\tau.
+\]
+This proves exact unbiasedness of the oracle clipped score.

statements.statement: Let delta_1 = hat_g_1n - g_1 and delta_0 = hat_g_0n - g_0. Conditional on the training folds used to produce the cross-fitted nuisance estimates, P(hat_psi_lambda - psi_lambda) equals the sum of the treated and control regression drift terms, and it obeys the bound |P(hat_psi_lambda - psi_lambda)| <= pi(lambda)^(1/2) ||delta_1||_{L2(P_X)} + lambda^(-1) ||delta_1||_{L2(P_X)} ||hat_e_n - e||_{L2(P_X)} + (2 / eta_plus) ||delta_0||_{L2(P_X)} ||hat_e_n - e||_{L2(P_X)}.
statements.proof_tex:
Write $\delta_1=\hat g_{1n}-g_1$, $\delta_0=\hat g_{0n}-g_0$, and keep the notation $\hat e_\lambda(x)=\min\{1-\eta_+/2,\max\{\hat e_n(x),\lambda\}\}$. By cross-fitting, on each validation observation the fitted nuisance functions are measurable with respect to the training folds and are independent of the validation draw. Conditional on the training folds, they may therefore be treated as fixed functions.
+
+Expand the score difference:
+\[
+\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda
+=\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{D}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)
+-\delta_0(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{1-D}{1-\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)
++D\{Y-g_1(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}-\frac{1}{e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)
+-(1-D)\{Y-g_0(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{1-\hat e_\lambda(X)}-\frac{1}{1-e(X)}\Bigr).
+\]
+Taking conditional expectations given the training folds, the last two terms vanish because $\mathbb E[Y-g_1(X)\mid D=1,X]=0$ and $\mathbb E[Y-g_0(X)\mid D=0,X]=0$. Hence
+\[
+P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)
+=P\!\left[\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)\right]
+-P\!\left[\delta_0(X)\frac{e(X)-\hat e_\lambda(X)}{1-\hat e_\lambda(X)}\right].
+\]
+
+We bound the treated piece first. Split according to $\{e(X)\le \lambda\}$ and $\{e(X)>\lambda\}$. On $\{e\le\lambda\}$ we only use $|1-e/\hat e_\lambda|\le 1$, so Cauchy--Schwarz gives
+\[
+\left|P\!\left[\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)\mathbf 1\{e(X)\le \lambda\}\right]\right|
+\le P(e(X)\le \lambda)^{1/2}\,\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}
+=\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+On $\{e>\lambda\}$ we have $e_\lambda=e$, so
+\[
+1-\frac{e}{\hat e_\lambda}=\frac{\hat e_\lambda-e}{\hat e_\lambda},
+\]
+and because $\hat e_\lambda\ge \lambda$ pointwise,
+\[
+\left|1-\frac{e}{\hat e_\lambda}\right|\le \lambda^{-1}|\hat e_\lambda-e|.
+\]
+The clipping map $u\mapsto \min\{1-\eta_+/2,\max\{u,\lambda\}\}$ is $1$-Lipschitz, so $|\hat e_\lambda-e_\lambda|\le |\hat e_n-e|$ pointwise; on $\{e>\lambda\}$ we have $e_\lambda=e$. Therefore
+\[
+\left|P\!\left[\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)\mathbf 1\{e(X)>\lambda\}\right]\right|
+\le \lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}\,\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+
+For the control piece, right-overlap implies $e(X)\le 1-\eta_+$ almost surely, and the definition of $\hat e_\lambda$ gives $\hat e_\lambda(X)\le 1-\eta_+/2$. Thus $1-\hat e_\lambda(X)\ge \eta_+/2$, so
+\[
+\left|\frac{e(X)-\hat e_\lambda(X)}{1-\hat e_\lambda(X)}\right|
+\le \frac{2}{\eta_+}|e(X)-\hat e_\lambda(X)|
+\le \frac{2}{\eta_+}|\hat e_n(X)-e(X)|.
+\]
+Hence
+\[
+\left|P\!\left[\delta_0(X)\frac{e(X)-\hat e_\lambda(X)}{1-\hat e_\lambda(X)}\right]\right|
+\le \frac{2}{\eta_+}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}\,\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+
+Combining the three displays,
+\[
+|P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)|
+\le \pi(\lambda)^{1/2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\frac{2}{\eta_+}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+This is the exact drift decomposition needed for the feasible score.

statements.statement: Conditional on the training folds used for cross-fitting, the evaluation-sample fluctuation of the feasible score obeys hat_tau_lambda - P hat_psi_lambda = O_P(n^(-1/2) [ E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)]^(1/2) + lambda^(-1) ||hat_g_1n - g_1||_{L2(P_X)} + ||hat_g_0n - g_0||_{L2(P_X)} + lambda^(-3/2) ||hat_e_n - e||_{L2(P_X)} + ||hat_e_n - e||_{L2(P_X)} ]).
statements.proof_tex:
Continue to condition on the training folds, so that the fitted nuisance functions are fixed and independent of the validation sample. Let $\mathbb G_n f=n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^n\{f(O_i)-Pf\}$ for the evaluation sample. Then
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda=\mathbb G_n\hat\psi_\lambda.
+\]
+Using the decomposition from the previous lemma,
+\[
+\mathbb G_n\hat\psi_\lambda
+=\mathbb G_n\psi_\lambda+\mathbb G_n A_1-\mathbb G_n A_0+\mathbb G_n B_1-\mathbb G_n B_0,
+\]
+where
+\[
+A_1(O)=\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{D}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr),\qquad
+A_0(O)=\delta_0(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{1-D}{1-\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr),
+\]
+\[
+B_1(O)=D\{Y-g_1(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}-\frac{1}{e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr),\qquad
+B_0(O)=(1-D)\{Y-g_0(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{1-\hat e_\lambda(X)}-\frac{1}{1-e(X)}\Bigr).
+\]
+Each displayed summand is conditionally mean zero after centering by $P$.
+
+For the oracle term, $g_1$ and $g_0$ are square-integrable because they are assumed to be $L_2(P_X)$-estimable. Therefore
+\[
+P[\psi_\lambda^2]\le C_P\{1+E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\}
+\]
+for a finite constant $C_P$ depending only on $P$, $\sigma_1^2$, $\sigma_0^2$, and $\eta_+$. Indeed, the treated residual contribution satisfies
+\[
+P\left[\frac{D\{Y-g_1(X)\}^2}{e_\lambda(X)^2}\right]
+=E\left[\frac{e(X)\,E[(Y-g_1(X))^2\mid D=1,X]}{e_\lambda(X)^2}\right]
+\le \sigma_1^2 E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}],
+\]
+because $e(X)\le e_\lambda(X)$, and the control residual contribution is bounded by $\eta_+^{-2}\sigma_0^2$ since $1-e(X)\ge \eta_+$. Hence conditional Chebyshev yields
+\[
+\mathbb G_n\psi_\lambda=O_P\!\left(n^{-1/2}(1+E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}])^{1/2}\right)
+=O_P\!\left(n^{-1/2}E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}\right),
+\]
+because $E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\ge 1$.
+
+For $A_1$, use the crude pointwise bound $|1-D/\hat e_\lambda|\le 1+\lambda^{-1}\le 2\lambda^{-1}$. Hence
+\[
+P[A_1^2]\le 4\lambda^{-2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}^2,
+\]
+so
+\[
+\mathbb G_n A_1=O_P\!\left(n^{-1/2}\lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}\right).
+\]
+
+For $A_0$, right-overlap and the upper clipping imply $1-\hat e_\lambda\ge \eta_+/2$, hence
+\[
+\left|1-\frac{1-D}{1-\hat e_\lambda}\right|\le 1+\frac{2}{\eta_+},
+\]
+so
+\[
+P[A_0^2]\le C_{\eta_+}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}^2,
+\qquad
+\mathbb G_n A_0=O_P\!\left(n^{-1/2}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}\right).
+\]
+
+For $B_1$, the map $u\mapsto 1/u$ is $\lambda^{-2}$-Lipschitz on $[\lambda,1]$, so
+\[
+\left|\frac{1}{\hat e_\lambda}-\frac{1}{e_\lambda}\right|
+\le \lambda^{-2}|\hat e_\lambda-e_\lambda|
+\le \lambda^{-2}|\hat e_n-e|.
+\]
+Therefore, using the treated residual envelope,
+\[
+P[B_1^2]
+\le \sigma_1^2 E\left[e(X)\lambda^{-4}|\hat e_n(X)-e(X)|^2\right]
+\le \sigma_1^2\lambda^{-3}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}^2,
+\]
+because $e(X)\le e_\lambda(X)$ and $e_\lambda(X)\ge \lambda$. Thus
+\[
+\mathbb G_n B_1=O_P\!\left(n^{-1/2}\lambda^{-3/2}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}\right).
+\]
+
+For $B_0$, the map $u\mapsto (1-u)^{-1}$ is Lipschitz on $(-\infty,1-\eta_+/2]$ with constant $4\eta_+^{-2}$. Hence
+\[
+\left|\frac{1}{1-\hat e_\lambda}-\frac{1}{1-e}\right|
+\le C_{\eta_+}|\hat e_n-e|,
+\]
+and the control residual envelope gives
+\[
+P[B_0^2]\le C_{\eta_+,\sigma_0^2}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}^2,
+\qquad
+\mathbb G_n B_0=O_P\!\left(n^{-1/2}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}\right).
+\]
+
+Summing the five centered pieces,
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda
+=O_P\!\Bigl(n^{-1/2}\bigl[E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}
++\lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\lambda^{-3/2}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}\bigr]\Bigr).
+\]
+This is the fluctuation bound available from the frozen assumptions.

statements.statement: Under the witness-rate hypotheses in ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e, and for every deterministic lambda in Lambda_n, the feasible cross-fitted estimator satisfies |hat_tau_lambda - tau| = O_P( n^(-1/2) [ E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)]^(1/2) + lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en ] + pi(lambda)^(1/2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en ).
statements.proof_tex:
Assume the witness estimators satisfy
+\[
+\|\hat g_{1n}-g_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}=O_P(r_{1n}),\qquad
+\|\hat g_{0n}-g_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}=O_P(r_{0n}),\qquad
+\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}=O_P(r_{en}),
+\]
+as required by the estimability assumptions. By the decomposition
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-\tau
+=\bigl(\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda\bigr)
++P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)
++\bigl(P\psi_\lambda-\tau\bigr),
+\]
+Lemma \texttt{lem:oracle-clipped-score-unbiased} gives $P\psi_\lambda-\tau=0$. Lemma \texttt{lem:feasible-score-drift-decomposition} gives
+\[
+P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)
+=O_P\bigl(\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}r_{1n}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}\bigr),
+\]
+where the last term absorbs the fixed factor $2/\eta_+$. Lemma \texttt{lem:feasible-score-fluctuation-bound} gives
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda
+=O_P\!\Bigl(n^{-1/2}\bigl[E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en}\bigr]\Bigr).
+\]
+Combining the two displays yields
+\[
+|\hat\tau_\lambda-\tau|
+=O_P\!\Bigl(n^{-1/2}\bigl[E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en}\bigr]
++\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}r_{1n}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}\Bigr).
+\]
+This is a valid upper envelope under the frozen assumptions. It is strictly weaker than the target envelope $U_n(\lambda)$ because the target omits the displayed stochastic terms coming from the feasible estimated denominators.

statements.statement: Let \(\mathcal P\) be any class of laws for a sample of size \(n\). If \(P_0,P_1\in\mathcal P\) satisfy \(\chi^2(P_1^{\otimes n},P_0^{\otimes n})\le C_\chi<4\) and \(|\tau(P_1)-\tau(P_0)|\ge \Delta\), then \(\inf_{\widehat\tau}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P} E_P|\widehat\tau-\tau(P)|\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}(1-\sqrt{C_\chi}/2)\).
statements.proof_tex:
Let $\widehat\tau$ be any estimator based on $O_1,\dots,O_n$, and write $P_0^n:=P_0^{\otimes n}$ and $P_1^n:=P_1^{\otimes n}$. Set $\tau_j:=\tau(P_j)$ and $\Delta:=|\tau_1-\tau_0|$. For every sample point $z$ one has the deterministic inequality $|\widehat\tau(z)-\tau_0|+|\widehat\tau(z)-\tau_1|\ge \Delta$. Multiplying by $\min\{dP_0^n,dP_1^n\}$ and integrating gives
\[
E_{P_0^n}|\widehat\tau-\tau_0|+E_{P_1^n}|\widehat\tau-\tau_1|\ge \Delta\int \min\{dP_0^n,dP_1^n\}=\Delta\bigl(1-\operatorname{TV}(P_0^n,P_1^n)\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
\max\Bigl\{E_{P_0^n}|\widehat\tau-\tau_0|,E_{P_1^n}|\widehat\tau-\tau_1|\Bigr\}\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}\bigl(1-\operatorname{TV}(P_0^n,P_1^n)\bigr).
\]
Now use the standard inequality $\operatorname{TV}(Q,R)\le \tfrac12\sqrt{\chi^2(Q,R)}$. If $\chi^2(P_1^n,P_0^n)\le C_\chi<4$, then $\operatorname{TV}(P_0^n,P_1^n)\le \tfrac12\sqrt{C_\chi}<1$, so
\[
\max\Bigl\{E_{P_0^n}|\widehat\tau-\tau_0|,E_{P_1^n}|\widehat\tau-\tau_1|\Bigr\}\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}\Bigl(1-\frac{\sqrt{C_\chi}}{2}\Bigr).
\]
Taking the infimum over $\widehat\tau$ and using that $\{P_0,P_1\}\subseteq\mathcal P$ yields
\[
\inf_{\widehat\tau}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P}E_P|\widehat\tau-\tau(P)|\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}\Bigl(1-\frac{\sqrt{C_\chi}}{2}\Bigr).
\]
This is the claimed two-point lower bound.

statements.statement: Let \(\mathcal F=\{P_{s,t}:(s,t)\in\{-1,1\}^2\}\subseteq\mathcal P\). If every pair of product laws in \(\mathcal F\) satisfies \(\chi^2(P_{s,t}^{\otimes n},P_{s',t'}^{\otimes n})\le C_\chi<4\) and the parameter diameter obeys \(\max_{(s,t),(s',t')} |\tau(P_{s,t})-\tau(P_{s',t'})|\ge \Delta\), then \(\inf_{\widehat\tau}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P} E_P|\widehat\tau-\tau(P)|\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}(1-\sqrt{C_\chi}/2)\).
statements.proof_tex:
Let $\mathcal F=\{P_{s,t}:(s,t)\in\{-1,1\}^2\}\subseteq \mathcal P$ satisfy the stated assumptions, and let
\[
\Delta:=\max_{(s,t),(s',t')}|\tau(P_{s,t})-\tau(P_{s',t'})|.
\]
Choose indices $(\bar s,\bar t)$ and $(\bar s',\bar t')$ attaining this maximum. By the pairwise bound for the family, $\chi^2\!\left(P_{\bar s,\bar t}^{\otimes n},P_{\bar s',\bar t'}^{\otimes n}\right)\le C_\chi<4$. Applying Lemma~\textnormal{lem:two-point-le-cam-chi-square} to the two-point subfamily $\{P_{\bar s,\bar t},P_{\bar s',\bar t'}\}$ gives
\[
\inf_{\widehat\tau}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P}E_P|\widehat\tau-\tau(P)|\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}\Bigl(1-\frac{\sqrt{C_\chi}}{2}\Bigr).
\]
This is exactly the desired conclusion.

statements.statement: There exists a universal constant c > 0 such that inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| >= c R_n^*.
statements.proof_tex:
By definition, W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) is obtained from W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) by adding the extra member property ass:regular-cell-mass and keeping the other defining conditions the same. Hence W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) is a subclass of W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en).

Fix any estimator hat_tau based on O_1, ..., O_n. Since a supremum over a larger class dominates the supremum over a subclass,

sup_{P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| >= sup_{P in W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)|.

Taking the infimum over all estimators preserves the inequality, so

inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| >= inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)|.

Now apply conj:tail-lower-bound, which asserts that the right-hand side is at least c R_n^* for some universal constant c > 0. Therefore

inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| >= c R_n^*.

This proves the lower-bound half of the proposed frontier.

statements.statement: Fix exponents a_0,a_1,a_e>0 and specialize r_0n=n^(-a_0), r_1n=n^(-a_1), and r_en=n^(-a_e). If either kappa != 1, or kappa = 1 and nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1) < 1/2, then there exist constants 0 < c <= C < infinity, depending only on a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa, and lambda_0, such that c n^(-nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa)) <= R_n^* <= C n^(-nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa)).
statements.proof_tex:
Set
\[
m_\kappa(t)=
\begin{cases}
\min\!\left\{\frac12-\frac{1-\kappa}{2}t,\ a_1+\frac{\kappa}{2}t,\ a_1+a_e-t,\ a_0+a_e\right\}, & 0<\kappa<1,\\[0.5ex]
\min\!\left\{\frac12,\ a_1+\frac{\kappa}{2}t,\ a_1+a_e-t,\ a_0+a_e\right\}, & \kappa\ge 1.
\end{cases}
\]
By Definition `def:phase-exponent`, \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)=\sup_{t\ge 0} m_\kappa(t)\).

Assume first that \(\kappa\neq 1\). For \(\lambda=\lambda_0 n^{-t}\in\Lambda_n\), the frontier functional in Definition `def:frontier-functional` becomes
\[
R_n(\lambda_0 n^{-t})=
\begin{cases}
\lambda_0^{-(1-\kappa)/2}n^{-\{1/2-t(1-\kappa)/2\}}
+\lambda_0^{\kappa/2}n^{-\{a_1+t\kappa/2\}}
+\lambda_0^{-1}n^{-\{a_1+a_e-t\}}
+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}, & 0<\kappa<1,\\[0.8ex]
n^{-1/2}
+\lambda_0^{\kappa/2}n^{-\{a_1+t\kappa/2\}}
+\lambda_0^{-1}n^{-\{a_1+a_e-t\}}
+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}, & \kappa>1.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(\lambda_0\in(0,1)\) is fixed, there are constants \(0<c\le C<\infty\), depending only on \((a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa,\lambda_0)\), such that for every admissible \(t\),
\[
c\,n^{-m_\kappa(t)}\le R_n(\lambda_0 n^{-t})\le C\,n^{-m_\kappa(t)}.
\]
The function \(m_\kappa\) is continuous and piecewise affine on \([0,\infty)\), and because the term \(a_1+a_e-t\) tends to \(-\infty\) as \(t\to\infty\), the supremum in Definition `def:phase-exponent` is attained at some finite \(t_\star\ge 0\). Since \(\bar t_n\to\infty\), we have \(t_\star\le \bar t_n\) for all large \(n\), so \(\lambda_0 n^{-t_\star}\in\Lambda_n\). The upper bound at \(t_\star\) gives
\[
R_n^*\le R_n(\lambda_0 n^{-t_\star})\le C n^{-m_\kappa(t_\star)}=C n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Conversely, for every admissible \(t\), \(m_\kappa(t)\le \nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\), hence
\[
R_n(\lambda_0 n^{-t})\ge c n^{-m_\kappa(t)}\ge c n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Taking the infimum over \(\lambda\in\Lambda_n\) yields
\[
c n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}\le R_n^*\le C n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
This proves the claim when \(\kappa\neq 1\).

Now assume \(\kappa=1\) and \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2\). Write
\[
m_1(t)=\min\left\{\frac12,\ a_1+\frac t2,\ a_1+a_e-t,\ a_0+a_e\right\}.
\]
If there were some \(t\) with \(m_1(t)=1/2\), then the supremum defining \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)\) would be at least \(1/2\), contradicting \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2\). Therefore
\[
m_1(t)<1/2\qquad\text{for every }t\ge 0.
\]
For \(\lambda=\lambda_0 n^{-t}\), Definition `def:frontier-functional` gives
\[
R_n(\lambda_0 n^{-t})
= n^{-1/2}\{1+t\log n\}^{1/2}
+\lambda_0^{1/2}n^{-\{a_1+t/2\}}
+\lambda_0^{-1}n^{-\{a_1+a_e-t\}}
+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}.
\]
Since \(m_1(t)<1/2\), at least one of the last three exponents equals \(m_1(t)\). Hence there is a constant \(c>0\), depending only on \((a_0,a_1,a_e,\lambda_0)\), such that
\[
R_n(\lambda_0 n^{-t})\ge c n^{-m_1(t)}\ge c n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}
\qquad\text{for every admissible }t.
\]
Again \(m_1\) is continuous, piecewise affine, and tends to \(-\infty\) as \(t\to\infty\), so it attains its supremum at some finite \(t_\star\ge 0\). For this \(t_\star\),
\[
m_1(t_\star)=\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2.
\]
Therefore
\[
n^{-1/2}\{1+t_\star\log n\}^{1/2}=o\bigl(n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}\bigr),
\]
while each of the last three terms is \(O(n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)})\). Hence
\[
R_n(\lambda_0 n^{-t_\star})\le C n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}
\]
for all large \(n\), and taking the infimum over \(\Lambda_n\) gives the matching upper bound. Combining the two bounds proves
\[
c n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}\le R_n^*\le C n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}
\]
whenever \(\kappa=1\) and \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2\).

statements.statement: Let B = min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e}. Then nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa) <= B for every kappa > 0. If 0 < kappa < kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e), then nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa) < B.
statements.proof_tex:
Let
\[
B=\min\{1/2,\ a_1+a_e,\ a_0+a_e\}.
\]
We first show \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\le B\) for every \(\kappa>0\). If \(0<\kappa<1\), then for every \(t\ge 0\),
\[
\min\left\{\frac12-\frac{1-\kappa}{2}t,\ a_1+\frac{\kappa}{2}t,\ a_1+a_e-t,\ a_0+a_e\right\}
\le \min\{1/2,\ a_1+a_e,\ a_0+a_e\}=B,
\]
because the first entry is at most \(1/2\), the third is at most \(a_1+a_e\), and the fourth equals \(a_0+a_e\). Taking the supremum over \(t\ge 0\) gives \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\le B\). The same argument works when \(\kappa\ge 1\), because the defining minimum is then
\[
\min\left\{\frac12,\ a_1+\frac{\kappa}{2}t,\ a_1+a_e-t,\ a_0+a_e\right\},
\]
which is again bounded above by \(B\). Thus \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\le B\) for all \(\kappa>0\).

Now assume \(0<\kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)\). If we had \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)=B\), then this \(\kappa\) would belong to the set
\[
\{u>0:\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,u)=B\}
\]
whose infimum is, by Definition `def:critical-exponent`, exactly \(\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)\). But then
\[
\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)
\le \kappa
< \kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e),
\]
a contradiction. Therefore \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\neq B\). Since we already proved \(\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\le B\), it follows that
\[
\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)<B.
\]

sampling_model.design: i.i.d. observational study under unconfoundedness with one-sided overlap decay at e(X) = 0 and right overlap bounded away from one
sampling_model.overlap: The main class W_n is indexed by dyadic-shell tail mass P(lambda / 2 < e(X) <= lambda) of order lambda^kappa on (0, lambda_0], with e(X) <= 1 - eta_plus almost surely, 2 lambda_0 < 1 - eta_plus, and clipping thresholds restricted to Lambda_n = {lambda_0 n^(-t) : 0 <= t <= tbar_n}. The lower-bound route uses the subclass W_n^lb that also retains a fixed interior regular cell P(2 lambda_0 < e(X) <= 1 - eta_plus) >= c_reg. The bounded-overlap sanity branch is carried by the separate comparator class W_n^reg with e(X) >= eta_minus and e(X) <= 1 - eta_plus almost surely.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: Both W_n and W_n^reg are defined by existence of L2(P_X)-rate-optimal nuisance learners at rates r_1n, r_0n, and r_en; the upper theorem uses cross-fitted witness estimators and a one-sided clipped doubly robust score whose exact risk envelope is U_n(lambda).

estimand_functional: R_n^* = inf_{lambda in Lambda_n}{ n^(-1/2) v_kappa(lambda) + lambda^(kappa / 2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en }

tldr: Full-population ATE estimation is studied over one-sided weak-overlap classes where the left propensity tail satisfies P(e(X) <= lambda) of order lambda^kappa, the right tail stays regular, and the tail cap obeys 2 lambda_0 < 1 - eta_plus so the lower-bound regular cell is nonvacuous. The formal objects are an exact clipped-score upper envelope U_n(lambda), its tail-order surrogate R_n(lambda) with the kappa = 1 variance scale normalized as (1 + log(lambda_0 / lambda))^(1/2), a separate regular-overlap comparator class W_n^reg recovering the Jin-Syrgkanis and Bonvini-Kennedy-Dukes-Balakrishnan bounded-overlap benchmark, a comparator-separation proposition that formally states strict deterioration on the subclass kappa < kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e), and a lower-bound handle based on a clipped expit-logit propensity perturbation evaluated at a deterministic near-minimizer lambda_dagger_n. The flagship conjecture is that the minimax absolute ATE risk is equivalent to R_n^* = inf_lambda R_n(lambda), with the kappa = 1 logarithm appearing only in the root-n regime.

project_justification.gap: The closest regular-overlap comparators, JinSyrgkanis2024StructureAgnosticDR and BonviniKennedyDukesBalakrishnan2024StructureAgnosticATE, hold nuisance difficulty fixed but leave the overlap axis regular; ArmstrongKolesar2021FiniteSampleATE handles lack of overlap through fixed-design finite-sample optimality under explicit smoothness classes rather than a random-design structure-agnostic nuisance frontier; the closest weak-overlap comparators, Dorn2025WeakOverlapDRTStats, MaSantAnnaSasakiUra2023DRWeakOverlap, and MaWang2020RobustIPW, study thresholding, trimming, or inference rather than a matched minimax risk frontier for the original ATE; and the closest general converse comparator, JinSyrgkanis2025GeneralLinearFunctionalLowerBounds, is not specialized to a one-sided overlap-shell experiment.
project_justification.niche: The niche is the asymmetric regime where only e(X) -> 0 is irregular. In that regime the treated-score variance term and the treated-side nuisance-product term become tail-sensitive, while the control-side product channel remains regular-overlap-like and the regular-cell lower-bound geometry can be separated from the tail shell by 2 lambda_0 < 1 - eta_plus.
project_justification.fill: The project fills that niche with a comparator-tightened frontier: same full-population ATE and same structure-agnostic nuisance-rate inputs as the published bounded-overlap theory, but with a one-sided overlap-decay index kappa, an exact clipped-score upper envelope, a separate regular-overlap comparator class for the sanity reduction, and a Le Cam / Assouad lower-bound handle targeted at a deterministic near-minimizing shell so the converse speaks directly to R_n^*.

related_work: JinSyrgkanis2024StructureAgnosticDR is the exact bounded-overlap comparator. Its Introduction equation (1) defines ATE and ATT, equations (5)-(7) identify ATE and weighted ATE under m_0(X) in [c, 1 - c] almost surely, and equation (8) states the bounded-overlap structure-agnostic benchmark. Prop:strict-overlap-sanity uses the separate regular-overlap class W_n^reg to reduce back to that benchmark, while prop:comparator-separation is the formal statement that the one-sided shell model is a strict extension rather than a prose-level analogy. BonviniKennedyDukesBalakrishnan2024StructureAgnosticATE is the closest smoothness-aware ATE comparator because the abstract explicitly lists minimax lower bounds for the pure structure-agnostic class and for a hybrid smoothness class; the present kernel keeps the same full-population ATE and black-box nuisance language but replaces regular overlap by one-sided shell decay. ArmstrongKolesar2021FiniteSampleATE is a close lack-of-overlap comparator because its introduction studies finite-sample optimal ATE estimation and confidence intervals without overlap assumptions and derives minimum smoothness thresholds for root-n inference, but it conditions on the realized design and optimizes linear estimators over explicit smoothness classes rather than deriving a random-design structure-agnostic kappa-shell minimax frontier. Dorn2025WeakOverlapDRTStats is the nearest weak-overlap estimator comparator because its abstract gives thresholded doubly robust asymptotic normality together with nuisance-rate and outcome-smoothness costs under weak overlap; thm:clipped-upper converts that style of bookkeeping into an absolute-risk envelope rather than an inference theorem. MaSantAnnaSasakiUra2023DRWeakOverlap and MaWang2020RobustIPW remain trimming comparators because they preserve the original ATE or robustify inference under small denominators without supplying matched upper and lower absolute-risk laws. KennedyBalakrishnanRobinsWasserman2024MinimaxCATE is the closest converse template because its abstract says the lower bound is built by a localized fuzzy-hypotheses argument for pointwise CATE; conj:tail-lower-bound moves that localization from a covariate point to the propensity boundary for a global ATE. JinSyrgkanis2025GeneralLinearFunctionalLowerBounds is the closest newer converse comparator because its abstract first recovers the ATE case and then extends to general functionals in two debiasing regimes; conj:minimax-frontier asks for the one-sided overlap-shell specialization that is still absent at that paper-level scope. KhanTamer2010IrregularIdentification, HongLeungLi2020FinitePopulationLimitedOverlap, and HeilerKazak2021ExtremePropensity remain the irregularity background: they explain why inverse-weight tails alter rates or limit laws, but they do not produce the one-sided treated/control phase diagram or the strict comparator theorem encoded here. RobinsLiMukherjeeTchetgenVanDerVaart2017MinimaxHOIF remains the higher-order comparator because it leaves open whether smoothness gains survive one-sided overlap decay.
honest_scope: The exact clipped-score upper envelope U_n(lambda), the tail-order reduction comparing U_n(lambda) and R_n(lambda), the compatibility condition 2 lambda_0 < 1 - eta_plus, the separate regular-overlap comparator class W_n^reg, and the strict-overlap reduction to the bounded-overlap benchmark are the settled formal objects in the proposal. The open object is the converse: complete the Le Cam / Assouad handle on W_n^lb into a bounded-divergence family at a deterministic near-minimizer lambda_dagger_n and then prove the lower bound c R_n^*. The minimax-frontier conjecture, the phase diagram, and the weak-tail strict-deterioration half of prop:comparator-separation are conditional on that converse rather than assumed as class conditions.
